notable: Aegean island off the Troad; Apollo's island in the Iliad and the Greeks' feigned-withdrawal anchorage before the wooden horse.
Town guarding the narrowest point of the Dardanelles; Schliemann's landfall for the Hisarlık excavations.
Ionian island, kingdom of Odysseus and goal of the Odyssey's return voyage.
Ionian island identified with the Phaeacians' Scheria in the Odyssey; Corinthian colony of Korkyra.
Capital of Roman Dalmatia; Diocletian's fortified retirement palace beside it became the medieval city of Split.
Black Sea port; Milesian colony of Odessos, later the Orient Express's eastern sea-link terminus.
Indian Ocean island sultanate and slaving entrepôt; the launch point for the great Victorian East-African expeditions.
Arab-Swahili trading town on the eastern shore of Lake Tanganyika where Stanley found Livingstone in 1871.
Central depot of the Arab ivory-and-slave caravan route to the interior; staging post for Burton, Speke and Stanley.
Mainland Swahili-coast terminus of the caravan routes, opposite Zanzibar; departure point for inland expeditions.
River in the Troad where Alexander won his first set-piece victory over the Persian satraps in 334 BCE.
Coastal plain in Cilicia where Alexander routed Darius III in 333 BCE; later a Byzantine–Sasanian theatre.
Apulian battlefield where Hannibal destroyed a numerically superior Roman army in 216 BCE.
Umbrian lake on whose misty northern shore Hannibal ambushed and destroyed Flaminius's consular army in 217 BCE.
Barcid capital in Iberia founded by Hasdrubal; Hannibal's springboard for the march on Italy.
Town and peninsula commanding the European side of the Dardanelles; the Ottomans' first European foothold (1354).
Roman legionary fortress and provincial capital of Britannia Inferior; later Anglo-Scandinavian Jorvik and modern York.
Principal Roman cross-Channel port in Gaul and base of the Classis Britannica fleet; modern Boulogne-sur-Mer.
Auxiliary fort behind Hadrian's Wall, famous for the Vindolanda writing tablets — the oldest surviving handwriting in Britain.
Samian colony of Perinthos on the Sea of Marmara, refounded as Herakleia and a major Late-Roman Thracian centre.
Fortified lake-town in western Macedonia, a strategic Byzantine stronghold in the Komnenian Balkan campaigns.
Armenian capital and Sasanian-then-Arab administrative seat south of Yerevan; a focus of Heraclius's eastern campaign.
Principal western-Indian port of British India; renamed Mumbai in 1995.
Hooghly-river port and capital of British India until 1911; renamed Kolkata in 2001.
